rationale.R1.1: Delegation 'What do you think?' on a research excerpt centrally demands grasping the thesis (BFT evaluation fragmentation) and the contribution claim (ConSenseBench as a unified harness); understanding these is prerequisite to any opinion.
rationale.R1.2: The paper conflates throughput (a performance metric) with liveness (a safety-adjacent temporal guarantee); evaluating logical coherence of this operationalization is a natural target of 'what do you think?' applied to a methods paper.
rationale.R1.3: The claim that HotStuff's throughput lead constitutes 'evidence of superior liveness' is a direct evidence-to-claim inference that any general assessment would scrutinize.
rationale.R1.4: The paper asserts 'to our knowledge, the first such standardization' while simultaneously crediting Patel and Ousterhout (2021) with essentially the same measurement framework; novelty assessment is squarely in scope for any opinion on a research contribution.
rationale.R1.7: The paper both leans on and claims to supersede Patel and Ousterhout (2021) in the same paragraph — a citation/attribution tension that rises to load-bearing concern for any holistic opinion on the work.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the assessment focus primarily on technical/logical soundness of the claims — e.g., whether committed-throughput adequately operationalizes liveness and whether the novelty claim is defensible given the Patel & Ousterhout citation — or primarily on presentation quality such as writing clarity, narrative structure, and accessibility?
